content: Adiabatic free expansion of ideal gas must be
explanation:
In an adiabatic process, there is no heat exchange, so $$Q = 0.$$

In free expansion, the gas expands into a vacuum, meaning there is no external pressure doing work, so $$W = 0.$$

From the first law of thermodynamics, we have:

$$\Delta U = Q - W.$$

Substituting the values, we get:

$$\Delta U = 0 - 0 = 0.$$

For an ideal gas, the internal energy $$U$$ depends only on the temperature. Since $$\Delta U = 0$$, the temperature does not change during the process. This characteristic is described as an isothermal process.

Thus, the adiabatic free expansion of an ideal gas is isothermal, which corresponds to:

Option C: Isothermal

content: $360 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of a hydrocarbon diffuses in 30 minutes, while under the same conditions $360 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ gas diffuses in one hour. The molecular formula of the hydrocarbon is
explanation:
According to Graham's law

$$ \frac{R_d(\text { hydrocarbon })}{R_d\left(\mathrm{SO}_2\right)}=\sqrt{\frac{M W_{\left(\mathrm{SO}_2\right)}}{M W_{\text {Hydrocarbon }}}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

where, $R_d$ is rate of diffusion.

Substituting the value in Eq.(i),

$$ \frac{\frac{360}{30}}{\frac{360}{60}}=\sqrt{\frac{64}{M W_H}} $$

On solving we get, $M W_{\text {Hydrocarbon }}=16$.

Thus, among the given options, $\mathrm{CH}_4$ has molecular weight of 16 .

content: An LPG (Liquified Petroleum Gas) cylinder weighs 15.0 kg when empty. When full, it weighs 30.0 kg and shows a pressure of 3.0 atm . In the course of usage at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the mass of the full cylinder is reduced to $24 \cdot 2 \mathrm{~kg}$. The volume of the used gas in cubic metre at the normal usage condition ( atm and $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) is (assume LPG to be normal butane and it behaves ideally)
explanation:
Given data

  • Empty cylinder mass = $15.0\ \text{kg}$

  • Full cylinder mass = $30.0\ \text{kg}$

    ⟹ Mass of LPG (liquid + vapour) when full = $30.0 - 15.0 = 15.0\ \text{kg}$

  • During usage, cylinder mass = $24.2\ \text{kg}$

    ⟹ Mass of LPG remaining = $24.2 - 15.0 = 9.2\ \text{kg}$

  • Hence, mass of LPG used = $15.0 - 9.2 = 5.8\ \text{kg}$

  • Temperature $T = 27^{\circ}C = 300\ \text{K}$

  • Pressure for the gas used (usage condition) $P = 1\ \text{atm}$

  • Molecular weight of butane ($\mathrm{C_4H_{10}}$) = 58 g/mol = 0.058 kg/mol

  • Gas constant $R = 0.0821\ \mathrm{L\ atm\ mol^{-1}\ K^{-1}}=8.314\ \mathrm{J\ mol^{-1}\ K^{-1}}$

Step 1: Moles of gas used

$ n = \frac{\text{mass}}{M} = \frac{5.8}{0.058} = 100\ \text{mol} $

Step 2: Use ideal gas law for the used gas at 1 atm, 27°C

$ PV = nRT $

$ V = \frac{nRT}{P} $

$ V = \frac{100 \times 0.0821 \times 300}{1} = 2463\ \mathrm{L} $

$ V = 2.463\ \mathrm{m^3} $

✅ Answer: $V = 2.46\ \mathrm{m^3}$

Option D is correct.

content: The compressibility factor for a van der Waal gas at high pressure is
explanation:
The compressibility factor (Z) is a measure of how much a real gas deviates from ideal gas behavior. For an ideal gas, the compressibility factor is 1. For real gases, Z can be less than or greater than 1 depending on the conditions, such as pressure and temperature.

The van der Waals equation for real gases is given by:

$$ \left( P + \frac{a}{V_m^2} \right) (V_m - b) = RT $$

Where:

  • P is the pressure
  • Vm is the molar volume
  • R is the universal gas constant
  • T is the temperature
  • a and b are van der Waals constants for the gas

At high pressures, the term $$ \frac{a}{V_m^2} $$ becomes negligible, and the van der Waals equation can be approximated as:

$$ P (V_m - b) = RT $$

Rearranging for Vm, we get:

$$ V_m = \frac{RT}{P} + b $$

The compressibility factor Z is defined as:

$$ Z = \frac{P V_m}{RT} $$

Substituting the expression for Vm into the definition of Z, we get:

$$ Z = \frac{P \left( \frac{RT}{P} + b \right)}{RT} $$

Simplifying this expression gives us:

$$ Z = 1 + \frac{Pb}{RT} $$

Thus, the compressibility factor for a van der Waals gas at high pressure is:

Option B: $$ 1+\frac{\mathrm{Pb}}{\mathrm{RT}} $$

content: The root mean square (rms) speed of X$$_2$$ gas is x m/s at a given temperature. When the temperature is doubled, the X$$_2$$ molecules dissociated completely into atoms. The root mean square speed of the sample of gas then becomes (in m/s)
explanation:
Given,

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} T_1 & =T & T_2=2 T \\ M_1 & =M & M_2 & =\frac{M}{2} \\ v_{\text {rms }(1)} & =x \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} & v_{\text {rms }(2)} & =? \end{array}$$

On substituting the above values, we will get

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \frac{v_1}{v_2}=\sqrt{\frac{T_1}{M_1} \times \frac{M_2}{T_2}}=\sqrt{\frac{T}{M} \times \frac{M / 2}{2 T}}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \therefore \frac{x}{v_2}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow v_2=2 \times \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned}$$

content:
Case - 1 : An ideal gas of molecular weight M at temperature T.

Case - 2 : Another ideal gas of molecular weight 2M at temperature T/2.

Identify the correct statement in context of above two cases.

options.content: Average kinetic energy and average speed will be the same in the two cases.
Only average speed is halved in the second case.

explanation:
As temperature is directly proportional to kinetic energy, thus when temperature is halved the average kinetic energy will also be halved. Also, we know that average speed is given by

$$\begin{aligned} & V_{\mathrm{av}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 R T}{M}} \\ \text{or} \quad & V_{\mathrm{av}} \propto \sqrt{\frac{T}{M}} \end{aligned}$$

Case I $$V_{\mathrm{av}} \propto \sqrt{\frac{T}{M}}$$

Case II $$V_{\mathrm{av}} \propto \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \times 2 M}}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{T}{M}}$$

So, average speed is also halved.

content: The average speed of H2 at T1K is equal to that of O2 at T2K. The ratio T1 : T2 is

explanation:
Avogadro's law is valid for ideal gas.

According to Avogadro's law, volume of ideal gas is directly proportional to number of moles of gas at constant temperature and pressure.

content: Let (Crms)H2 is the r.m.s. speed of H2 at 150 K. At what temperature, the most probable speed of helium [Cmp)He] will be half of (Crms)H2 ?

Molecular velocities of two gases at the same temperature (T) are u<sub>1</sub> and u<sub>2</sub>. Their masses are m<sub>1</sub> and m<sub>2</sub> respectively. Which of the following expressions is correct at temperature T?
explanation:
Let two gases are A and B.

u1 = molecular velocity of gas A

u2 = molecular velocity of gas B

m1 = molecular mass of gas A

m2 = molecular mass of gas B

The root mean square velocity, $${u_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over M}} $$ (where, M = molecular mass, R = gas constant)

For gas A,

$${u_1} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over {{m_1}}}} $$

$$u_1^2 = {{3RT} \over {{m_1}}}$$

$$T = {{u_1^2{m_1}} \over {3R}}$$ .... (i)

For gas B,

$${u_2} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over {{m_2}}}} $$

$$u_2^2 = {{3RT} \over {{m_2}}}$$

$$T = {{u_2^2{m_2}} \over {3R}}$$ .... (ii)

From Eq. (i) and (ii),

$${{u_1^2{m_1}} \over {3R}} = {{u_2^2{m_2}} \over {3R}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{m_1}u_1^2}$$ = $${{m_2}u_2^2}$$

content: At 273 K temperature and 76 cm Hg pressure the density of a gas is 1.964 g L<sup>-1</sup>. The gas is
explanation:
Given,

Density = 1.964 g/L

Pressure = 76 cm Hg = 1 atm

We know that density,

$$d = {{RT} \over {RM}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$M = {{0.0821\,L\,atm/K\,mol \times 273K} \over {1\,atm \times 1.964\,g/L}}$$

= 44 g/mol

Among the given gases, CO2 gas has molecular mass of 44 g/mol.

So, option (d) is correct.

content: For a van der Waals' gas, the term $$\left( {{{ab} \over {{V^2}}}} \right)$$ represents some
explanation:
Dimensions of $$\left( {{{ab} \over {{V^2}}}} \right)$$ are shown below.

$${a \over {{V^2}}} \times b$$ = pressure $$\times$$ volume

$$ = {{force} \over {area}} \times volume$$

$$ = {{ML{T^{ - 2}}} \over {{L^2}}} \times {L^3} = M{L^2}{T^{ - 2}}$$

Therefore, the dimensions of $$\left( {{{ab} \over {{V^2}}}} \right)$$ is same as that of energy. So, the option (b) is correct.

content: For same mass of two different ideal gases of molecular weights M1 and M2, Plots of log V vs log p at a given constant temperature are shown. Identify the correct option.

options.content: Can be predicted only if temperature is known

explanation:
For ideal gas, $$\log {k \over {{M_2}}},\log {k \over {{M_1}}}$$


$$\because$$ $$pV = {W \over M}RT$$ ..... (i)

Let WRT = K

Taking log of both the sides of Eq. (i)

$$\log p + \log V = \log {K \over M}$$

or $$\log V = - \log p + \log {K \over M}$$ ..... (ii)

[On comparing Eq. (ii) with y = mx + C]

$$\log {K \over {{M_2}}} > \log {K \over {{M_1}}}$$ or M1 > M2

content: Amongst the following, which should have the highest rms speed at the same temperature?
explanation: $$\because$$ rms velocity $$({v_{rms}}) \propto \sqrt {{1 \over M}} $$ Thus, smaller the mass, more will be the rms speed. H<sub>2</sub> has minimum molar mass. So it has highest value of rms speed.

content: Which statement(s) is/are applicable above critical temperature?

options.content: Surface tension of a liquid is very high.
A liq. phase cannot be distinguished from a gas phase.
Density changes continuously with P or V.

explanation: Statement given in option (a) is applicable above critical temperature. That is a gas cannot be liquefied above critical temperature, a liquid phase cannot be distinguished from a gas phase and density changes with $$p$$ or $$V$$.
